messages.content: I am making a c++ SDL game engine, I need help with the Renderer class, help me get it started. It has to be a full replacement for the SDL_renderer object.
I am dubious on calling it Render in a Renderer class, isn't that a bit redundant? Also would it make more sense calling it Update similar to Update method in XNA/Monogame or it is not related to it?

Oki you bring valid points, I will keep it as Render, but I will probably mix it with Draw methods inside other classes like SpriteSheet or Image classes, so if that doesn't mind then I will go with it. Another question I have is, should Clear, Present, be private or keeping it public is ok? Don't know if besides Render method what other uses of them outside it

OK I have the methods done, I want it to be similar to SpriteBatch class, what else do I need to implement? Should I make it similar or just avoid that since it is another library and doesn't has anything to do with XNA?

Thanks for the clean and useful feedback, I want to start with the minimum and iteratively add new features, so far I don't have any render targets, nor I know what are those, but I will keep this in mind when dealing with the viewports, layours, scaling textures, etc.
I also thought the texture scaled with the size of the window, but I guess I will have to keep that in mind when I make the SpriteSheet and Animation classes

You're welcome, and taking an iterative approach is a wise decision. This allows you to build a strong foundation for your engine and add complexity only when necessary.

Here's a brief explanation of render targets and a few considerations regarding scaling:

### Render Targets
Render targets are textures that you can draw onto instead of drawing directly to the screen. After rendering to the render target, you can then draw the render target to the screen as a texture. This is useful for a variety of effects, such as:

- Rendering scenes at a different resolution and then scaling them up/down to fit the window (useful for pixel art games or resolution-independent rendering).
- Post-processing effects (bloom, blur, etc.).
- Render-to-texture, which allows you to create dynamic textures based on your rendering (useful for mirrors, security cameras, or "rendering" text to a texture for UI).

In SDL, you would typically create a render target using `SDL_CreateTexture` with the `SDL_TEXTUREACCESS_TARGET` flag and then set it as the render target with `SDL_SetRenderTarget`.

### Window Scaling and Textures
Textures don't inherently scale with the window; you need to manage this yourself. The way a texture gets rendered to fit into the window depends on your draw call and the renderer's logical size or viewport configuration. Consider the following:

- **Logical Scaling**: You can define a logical size for rendering using `SDL_RenderSetLogicalSize` which SDL automatically scales to fit the window, maintaining aspect ratio.
- **Viewport Transforms**: You can also choose to use viewports (`SDL_RenderSetViewport`) to define where and how content will be scaled/drawn within the window.

### SpriteSheet and Animation Classes
When you implement `SpriteSheet` and `Animation` classes, manual texture scaling will likely be part of the equation. Spritesheets often hold multiple animation frames or various different graphics; you'll use source rectangles to select specific portions of the texture.

For animations, you'll likely progress through these frames over time and, depending on the resolution of your game and the window size, you may need to scale the resulting draw call to fit the desired on-screen size. 

#### Examples of Scaling Considerations:

- Fixed Resolution: Render everything to a fixed resolution and upscale/downscale to fit the window.
- Resolution-Independent: Scale all draw calls based on the window size or a dynamic camera zoom level.
- Pixel-Perfect: Especially important in pixel art games, where you want to ensure that each "pixel" in the texture maps to a specific number of screen pixels without distortion.

By focusing on the fundamentals and progressively building your sprite rendering, animation, and scaling logic, you will develop a better understanding of the low-level rendering details, which will serve you well as you expand your engine's capabilities. And always remember, rigorous testing is key when implementing these features to ensure they behave as expected under different scenarios.

Thanks I will keep that in mind! I have a new question, should I use SDL_Log like for example here: SDL_Log("ERROR: Failed to switch mode: %s\n", SDL_GetError()); if I don't want to expose the SDL library to the game itself? Should I just throw an exception here with that text instead?
Should I make a Logger class even thought it is so early in development, should I just use write line in the terminal or just leave it a commentary for now?

Ok I have everything set up now, but I have a question, I see these code snippets in the SDL sample project: if (SDL.SDL_Init(SDL.SDL_INIT_VIDEO) < 0) {...} and SDL_Quit(); Where should I place these things that are related to SDL and not directly to the Window or the Renderer? Should I create another class? If so, should it be a singleton to initialize or what? I think I am missing a link that connect everything together, maybe an Engine class?

I want the main game loop to be in the game, not the engine, since the game will use parts of the engine to work, so I don't know how to manage this, since the Run method shouldn't be in the Engine itself since the developer is going to be interacting with each engine subsystem separately

OK I am slowly getting the idea on this, say I have every subsystem as Singleton global objects, so ScreenManager, AudioManager, InputManager, etc. and they don't need to be initialized since they are static and you can just use their code without an object, also they have their own constructor (getinstance) and destructor, how would that change the Engine class?
